archetype: Real-Time Ground Station Antenna Scheduling & RF Interference Mitigation System for LEO Mega-Constellations
description: Orchestrates contact windows for thousands of low-Earth orbit satellites across a global network of ground antennas, dynamically adjusting schedules based on orbital predictions, regulatory frequency licensing, and real-time RF interference detection to maximize data downlink while preventing harmful interference. It is technically hard due to the combinatorial optimization problem of scheduling, the need for sub-second reaction to orbital decay and space weather, and strict adherence to international spectrum allocation treaties.
rules: MUST enforce schedule compliance with ITU Radio Regulations and national filings (e.g., FCC Part 25, Ofcom) via automated validation against a master spectrum license database.
MUST ingest and propagate orbital elements using SGP4/SDP4 models, with real-time updates from tracking data and space weather alerts (e.g., from NOAA SWPC).
MUST integrate with RF spectrum sensors and employ real-time interference geolocation (using TDOA/FDOA) to暂停 and re-route contacts upon detection of unauthorized emissions.
MUST generate and archive contact plans in CCSDS 503.0-B-2 (Cross Support Plan) format for interoperability between operator and ground station networks.
MUST implement a deterministic, fault-tolerant state machine for antenna command sequencing, with hard real-time deadlines for handover between contacts.
anti_patterns: DO NOT use a static, pre-computed weekly schedule—LEO orbits decay and drift, making static schedules obsolete within hours and causing missed contacts.
DO NOT ignore regulatory filing data; scheduling a transmission on a frequency not licensed for a specific orbital slot or geographic beam is a violation with severe penalties.
DO NOT treat antennas as simple sockets—each antenna's azimuth/elevation rate limits, frequency band, and tracking algorithm must be modeled as a first-class constraint.
key_technical_challenges: Solving the multi-objective, dynamic scheduling problem (thousands of satellites, hundreds of antennas, milliseconds of precision) under changing constraints (weather, maintenance, interference) requires hybrid constraint programming and heuristic algorithms.
Achieving sub-second re-scheduling in response to a detected interference event or a satellite's emergency maneuver while maintaining schedule stability for other contacts.

Creating a unified data model that harmonizes disparate orbital element formats (TLE, OEM), antenna control protocols (SCPS, CCSDS R&O), and spectrum license databases from multiple national regulators.

recommended_stack.language: Rust (for performance-critical scheduling core & antenna control) + Python (for data ingest, analysis, and API glue)
recommended_stack.framework: Actix/axum for high-performance Rust HTTP/gRPC services, with a separate Celery/Arq task queue for async orbital propagation jobs
recommended_stack.database_engine: TimescaleDB (hypertables for time-series telemetry & pass predictions) + PostGIS (for antenna site spatial queries & coverage polygons)
recommended_stack.cache: Redis (for real-time schedule state, antenna lock management, and fast lookup of active contact parameters)
recommended_stack.message_broker: Apache Kafka (for immutable event streams of orbital updates, interference alerts, and schedule change events)
special_services: Custom SGP4/SDP4 propagator service with space weather drag model integration (e.g., using NRL MSISE-00)
Regulatory License Compliance Microservice that maps ITU footnotes and national allocations to frequency/timestep constraints
RF Interference Detection & Geolocation Adapter that ingests data from spectrum analyzers (e.g., using RTSA formats) and triggers schedule abort

compliance_flags: ITU Radio Regulations (RR) Articles 9-11 (Frequency Allocation & Notification)
National Licensing Regulations (e.g., US FCC Part 25, UK Ofcom WTA)
CCSDS 503.0-B-2 (Cross Support Planning) for inter-operator coordination
